nativeRange.habitat: Rocky and coral reefs; benthic dweller on rocks, sand, or rubble in marine and occasionally brackish coastal waters

diet.notes: An ambush predator that generally feeds on small fish and crustaceans by luring prey with a modified dorsal spine (illicium) tipped with a fleshy lure (esca). In captivity, live feeder shrimp are often needed initially; many individuals can be trained over time to accept frozen foods such as krill, mysis shrimp, and small pieces of fish. Feeding every 3–5 days is generally advised, as overfeeding has been reported to cause refusal of food.

goodWith: fish too large to be swallowed

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Manuae, 4000x3000. Striking vibrant orange-red hairy morph in-situ at Lembeh Strait; shaggy dermal filaments and worm-like esca lure clearly visible; most colourful and diagnostic of the set.
Photo by Steve Childs, 2832x2306. Tack-sharp whole-body shot of the black hairy morph on sand; clearly shows the branched spinules/filaments characteristic of A. striatus.
Photo by Christian Gloor, 2048x1536. Dramatic pink/tan hairy morph gaping wide against black background; sharp, whole animal. Minor light corner watermark (why not recommended).

summary: An unusual ambush predator known for its remarkable camouflage and lure-fishing behavior. Generally advised for intermediate aquarists; best kept singly and away from small fish or crustaceans that could be viewed as prey.
careNotes:
The Striated Tiger Colored Angler (Antennarius striatus) is a highly camouflaged ambush predator capable of altering its coloration to match its surroundings remarkably well. It hunts by wiggling a modified first dorsal spine — tipped with a fleshy, worm-like lure called an esca — to draw small fish and crustaceans within striking distance, then lunges forward with a rapid gape to swallow prey whole. The strike is among the fastest of any vertebrate, and this species has been documented swallowing prey nearly equal to its own body size.

The Striated Tiger Colored Angler is generally considered reef-safe with respect to corals and sessile invertebrates, but it will attempt to eat any crustacean or fish small enough to fit in its mouth — including tank mates. Tankmates should be chosen with this in mind: companions large enough that they cannot be swallowed are the safest choice. Conspecific aggression tends to be high, so keeping more than one individual is generally not advised except in very large systems.

This species carries a venomous third dorsal spine that has been reported to inflict intense pain — described as comparable to a wasp sting — if mishandled. Use a container or wet net when moving the fish rather than bare hands.

The species' native range spans both temperate and tropical waters across the Indo-Pacific and Atlantic, which contributes to a relatively broad temperature tolerance in the aquarium. A tank of at least 170 litres (approximately 45 gallons) is generally recommended, though larger individuals benefit from more space.
breedingNotes: Oviparous; wild individuals deposit eggs in ribbon-like gelatinous masses. Males have been reported to display more intense coloration and extended skin appendages during courtship. Captive breeding has been documented but is generally considered rare and difficult to replicate intentionally.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is documented but uncommon. Eggs are released in ribbon-like gelatinous masses, and males have been reported to display intensified coloration during courtship.
